expanded_description: Measures of center and spread provide numerical summaries that describe the central tendency and variability of a data set. On the SAT, questions test the ability to compute and interpret the mean (average), median (middle value), mode (most frequent value), range (difference between maximum and minimum), and standard deviation (a measure of spread around the mean). These questions also test conceptual understanding: how adding or removing data points affects each measure, how the shape of a distribution relates to the relative positions of the mean and median, and which measure is most appropriate to report in a given context. Mastering this topic requires both computational fluency and the conceptual insight to reason about data distributions without necessarily performing full calculations.

core_concept.principle:
A data set is a collection of values, and summary statistics reduce this collection to a few numbers that capture its essential features. Measures of center—mean, median, and mode—indicate where the data is concentrated. Measures of spread—range, interquartile range, and standard deviation—indicate how widely the data is dispersed. Together, center and spread provide a compact description of the distribution's location and variability.

The definitions and properties of the major summary statistics are:

(1) Mean ($\bar{x}$): The arithmetic average, computed as the sum of all data values divided by the number of values. $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$. The mean is sensitive to extreme values (outliers); a single very large or very small value can pull the mean substantially away from the center of the bulk of the data. For skewed distributions, the mean is pulled in the direction of the tail.

(2) Median: The middle value when the data is arranged in order. For an odd number of data points, the median is the center value; for an even number, it is the average of the two center values. The median is resistant to outliers—extreme values do not affect its position, making it a better measure of center for skewed distributions.

(3) Mode: The most frequently occurring value(s) in the data set. A data set may have one mode (unimodal), more than one mode (bimodal or multimodal), or no mode if all values occur equally often. The mode is the only measure of center applicable to categorical data.

(4) Range: The difference between the maximum and minimum values. Range is a simple measure of total spread but is highly sensitive to outliers—a single extreme value can make the range very large even if the rest of the data is tightly clustered.

(5) Standard Deviation: A measure of the average distance of data points from the mean. A larger standard deviation indicates greater variability; a smaller standard deviation indicates that data points cluster more tightly around the mean. The SAT does not require computing standard deviation from a formula but tests conceptual understanding: comparing standard deviations of different data sets, understanding how adding or removing data affects variability, and interpreting standard deviation in context.

(6) Effect of Transformations: Adding a constant to every data value shifts the mean and median by that constant but does not change the range or standard deviation (the spread is unchanged). Multiplying every value by a constant multiplies the mean, median, range, and standard deviation by that constant.
core_concept.explanation:
Consider the data set: $\{3, 5, 7, 8, 12\}$. The mean is $(3 + 5 + 7 + 8 + 12) / 5 = 35 / 5 = 7$. The median (middle of the ordered list) is $7$. The mode does not exist (no repeated values). The range is $12 - 3 = 9$. The data is roughly symmetric around $7$.

Now add an outlier: $\{3, 5, 7, 8, 12, 95\}$. The mean becomes $(3 + 5 + 7 + 8 + 12 + 95) / 6 = 130 / 6 \approx 21.67$. The mean has been pulled far above the center of the original data. The median of six ordered values is the average of the third and fourth: $(7 + 8) / 2 = 7.5$. The median barely changed. The range becomes $95 - 3 = 92$, dramatically increased by the outlier. This illustrates the sensitivity of the mean and range to outliers and the robustness of the median.

For skewed distributions, the mean is pulled toward the tail. In a right-skewed distribution (long tail to the right), the mean is greater than the median. In a left-skewed distribution (long tail to the left), the mean is less than the median. The SAT may ask students to determine which is larger, the mean or the median, based on the shape of a distribution shown in a graph.

To compare standard deviations without calculation, look at the spread of the data. Two data sets with the same mean but different variabilities will have different standard deviations. If Set A is $\{4, 5, 6\}$ and Set B is $\{1, 5, 9\}$, both have mean $5$, but Set B has greater spread and therefore a larger standard deviation. If every value in a set is identical, the standard deviation is zero.

image.alt: Three graphs showing distributions with the mean and median marked. Left: symmetric (mean = median). Center: right-skewed (mean > median). Right: left-skewed (mean < median). Below, two dot plots comparing data sets with the same mean but different standard deviations.
image.caption: Measures of center and spread summarize data distributions. The mean is sensitive to outliers and skew; the median is resistant. Standard deviation quantifies variability around the mean.

sections.section_title: Foundation: Computing and Interpreting Summary Statistics
sections.introduction: Before students can answer SAT questions on measures of center and spread, they must know how to compute the mean, median, mode, and range, and they must understand the conceptual properties of these measures, particularly their behavior under transformations and in the presence of outliers.

key_concept.definition:
The mean, median, and mode are each measures of the 'center' of a data set, but they define center differently and are appropriate in different situations. The mean is the balance point of the data—the value such that the sum of deviations above equals the sum of deviations below. The median is the middle point—half the data is below, half above. The mode is the most common value. Understanding when each is the best description of a data set's center is a key conceptual skill.

Key properties:
- For a perfectly symmetric distribution, mean = median (assuming a single peak).
- The mean is pulled in the direction of skew or outliers; the median is not.
- Adding a constant $c$ to every value adds $c$ to the mean and median; the range and standard deviation are unchanged.
- Multiplying every value by a constant $k$ multiplies the mean, median, range, and standard deviation by $|k|$.
- The standard deviation is always non-negative and is zero only when all values are identical.
key_concept.context: SAT questions may ask for the computation of a specific measure, for a comparison of measures across data sets, or for the effect of a change in the data on the summary statistics. The Calculator section may involve larger data sets where computation of the mean is a test of careful arithmetic.

categories.description: Formulas and procedures for calculating mean, median, and range.

examples.explanation:
Sum all data values and divide by the count. For a frequency table, multiply each value by its frequency, sum these products, and divide by the total frequency.

Example: The scores on a test are $72, 85, 90, 88, 76, 95, 84$. The mean is $(72 + 85 + 90 + 88 + 76 + 95 + 84) / 7 = 590 / 7 \approx 84.29$.

Order the data from least to greatest. If $n$ is odd, the median is the value at position $(n + 1) / 2$. If $n$ is even, the median is the average of the values at positions $n/2$ and $(n/2) + 1$.

Example (odd): Scores $72, 76, 84, 85, 88, 90, 95$. $n = 7$, median position $(7+1)/2 = 4$th value, which is $85$.
Example (even): Scores $72, 76, 84, 85, 88, 90$. $n = 6$, median is average of 3rd and 4th: $(84 + 85) / 2 = 84.5$.

Range = maximum value − minimum value.

Example: For scores $72, 85, 90, 88, 76, 95, 84$, the maximum is $95$, the minimum is $72$. Range $= 95 - 72 = 23$.

worked_examples.title: Effect of Adding Data on Summary Statistics
worked_examples.problem: A data set contains the values $\{10, 12, 15, 18, 20\}$. (a) Find the mean, median, and range. (b) If the value $50$ is added to the data set, find the new mean, median, and range. (c) Compare the effect on each measure and explain why they differ.

step_by_step.explanation: Mean: $(10 + 12 + 15 + 18 + 20) / 5 = 75 / 5 = 15$. Median (ordered): middle of five values is the third, $15$. Range: $20 - 10 = 10$.

step_by_step.step: Step 2: Summary Statistics with Outlier Added
step_by_step.explanation: New data set: $\{10, 12, 15, 18, 20, 50\}$. Mean: $(10 + 12 + 15 + 18 + 20 + 50) / 6 = 125 / 6 \approx 20.83$. Increase of about $5.83$. Median: six values, median is average of 3rd and 4th: $(15 + 18) / 2 = 16.5$. Increase of $1.5$. Range: $50 - 10 = 40$. Increase of $30$.

The mean increased substantially, pulled toward the outlier. The median increased only slightly—it remained near the center of the original data. The range more than tripled, highly sensitive to the extreme value. This demonstrates the robustness of the median and the sensitivity of the mean and range to outliers.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Mean $= 15$, median $= 15$, range $= 10$. (b) With $50$ added: mean $\approx 20.83$, median $= 16.5$, range $= 40$. (c) The mean and range are strongly affected by the outlier; the median is relatively unaffected. This illustrates why the median is often preferred for skewed data or data with outliers.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding how outliers affect summary statistics is essential for selecting the appropriate measure for a given context and for interpreting reported statistics critically.

image.alt: A dot plot of the original data and the data with the outlier added. The mean and median are marked on each, showing how the mean shifts toward the outlier while the median barely moves.
image.caption: The mean is pulled toward outliers; the median stays near the center of the data

sections.section_title: Application: SAT Center and Spread Questions
sections.introduction: This section provides practice with the specific formats of SAT questions that test measures of center and spread. Problems include computing summary statistics, comparing distributions, and reasoning about the effects of data changes.

key_concept.definition: SAT center and spread questions test both computation and conceptual understanding. The strategic approach: (1) For computation, apply the formulas systematically. (2) For comparison, look at the shape of the distribution or the specific data values. (3) For questions about the effect of changes, reason from the properties of each measure (sensitivity to outliers, transformation rules). (4) For standard deviation, compare spread visually—wider spread means larger standard deviation—or reason about the effect of adding or removing extreme values.
key_concept.context: These questions appear primarily in the Calculator section, where larger data sets may be presented, but conceptual questions also appear in the No Calculator section.

categories.type: Practice: SAT-Style Center and Spread Problems
categories.description: Problems requiring computation, comparison, and conceptual reasoning.

examples.process: Problem 1: Mean vs. Median in Skewed Distribution
examples.explanation:
Question: A small company has $20$ employees. The salaries are right-skewed, with most employees earning between \$40,000 and \$60,000, but the CEO earns \$500,000. Which is larger, the mean salary or the median salary?

Solution: In a right-skewed distribution, the mean is pulled in the direction of the tail—toward the high CEO salary. The mean will be larger than the median. The median will be within the \$40,000-\$60,000 range, representing a typical employee. The mean will be inflated by the CEO's salary. Without calculating, we know the mean exceeds the median.

examples.process: Problem 2: Effect of Adding a Constant
examples.explanation:
Question: A data set has a mean of $25$ and a standard deviation of $4$. If $3$ is added to every value in the data set, what are the new mean and standard deviation?

Solution: Adding a constant shifts all values uniformly. The mean increases by the constant: new mean $= 25 + 3 = 28$. The spread (differences from the mean) is unchanged, so the standard deviation remains $4$.

image.alt: Two sample SAT problems: one conceptual (mean vs. median in skewed data), one transformational (effect of adding a constant).
image.caption: SAT center and spread questions test both computation and the conceptual understanding of how summary statistics behave

summary:
Measures of center and spread are the fundamental descriptive statistics that summarize data distributions. The mean, median, and mode describe central tendency in different ways, each with its own strengths and weaknesses. The range and standard deviation describe variability. The SAT tests both the calculation of these measures and the conceptual understanding of their properties—how they are affected by outliers, how they relate to the shape of a distribution, and how they transform when data is shifted or scaled.

The most important conceptual insights are the sensitivity of the mean and range to outliers and skew, and the consequent robustness of the median. In a skewed distribution, the mean is pulled toward the tail, making the median a better representation of a 'typical' value. The standard deviation, like the range, measures spread, but it does so in a way that considers all data points, not just the extremes. Understanding which measure is appropriate for which purpose—and being able to reason about how changes to data affect each measure—is the mark of statistical literacy that the SAT assesses.
